content:
In React, this behavior is called mounting or rendering a React tree into a root DOM container. A typical React app starts with HTML like this:

This is already in the HTML

Then JavaScript does this: import { createRoot } from "react-dom/client"; import App from "./App";const root = createRoot(document.getElementById("root"));root.render();The important idea is this: document.getElementById("root") gives React a real DOM element from the HTML page. That element is called the root container. When you call: root.render();you are telling React: “Use this DOM node as the place where my React application should live.” React does not prefer App because App is more important than the HTML. Instead, React has been explicitly instructed to manage the contents of that root element. Once React takes control of that container, the JSX returned by becomes the source of truth for what should appear inside #root. So if the original HTML had content inside #root, React will replace or take over that content with the UI produced by App. For example:

Original HTML content

and: root.render();where App is: function App() { return

Hello from React

; }The browser will display:

Hello from React

The outer div#root still exists. React does not remove the root container itself. React manages what is inside it. A good mental model is:
React controls this area
The #root element is like an empty slot in the HTML document. React fills that slot with the component tree starting from App. This is why App is often called the root component. It is the top component of your React component tree: #root DOM element └── ├──
├──
└──
There are two “roots” here, which can be confusing: HTML root container:
React root component: They are different things. The HTML root container is a real DOM element that already exists in index.html. The React root component is the first React component rendered into that container. Technically, this line creates a React root: const root = createRoot(document.getElementById("root"));This line renders the React component tree into that root: root.render();After this, React is responsible for keeping the DOM inside #root synchronized with your component output. If your component changes because of state, props, or re-rendering, React calculates what changed and updates the DOM efficiently. As a student, the main lesson is: Do not place important manual HTML inside the React root container if React is going to render there. This is fine:
This is also fine:
This is outside React
This is also outside React
But this is usually not useful:

I expect this to stay visible

because React will control that area after root.render() runs. The deeper concept is declarative UI. In React, you do not usually write HTML into the page manually. Instead, you describe what the UI should look like using components, and React updates the DOM to match that description. So the behavior you observed is not an error. It is the central React workflow: • HTML provides a container. • React attaches to that container. • App becomes the top-level component. • React controls the content inside the container. • The DOM updates whenever the React component tree changes. In short:
is the place where React enters the page. is the React application that gets placed there.
